data.title: Moderna extends YTD rally to hit 52-week high
data.description:
Moderna (MRNA) shares reached a new 52-week high on Thursday as the COVID-19 vaccine maker extended its recent gains to record a nearly 84% jump for this year alone after multiple years of consecutive declines.

The Cambridge, Massachusetts-based biotech last recorded an annual rally in 2021 when its shares more than doubled on the back of how its COVID-19 vaccine, Spikevax, which was the second to receive U.S. authorization, contributed to fighting the pandemic.

However, the stock recorded back-to-back annual losses in the subsequent years, and MRNA’s market capitalization plummeted from more than $180B in 2021 to roughly $12B by the end of 2025 as the pandemic’s impact on public health waned.

The upsurge comes as the company pivots its portfolio to other disease areas. Moderna (MRNA) was the third to receive U.S. authorization for a vaccine targeting respiratory syncytial virus ((RSV)) in 2025 when the FDA approved its RSV shot mRESVIA .

This week, the company and its partner Merck (MRK) posted encouraging long-term efficacy and safety data from a mid-stage trial for their jointly developed messenger RNA-based cancer vaccine, mRNA-4157, in certain patients with melanoma.

data.title: Intel shows mixed earnings revision, momentum metrics point towards strength
data.description:
Intel has rallied about 11% this week heading into its fourth quarter earnings release on Thursday.

Seeking Alpha's quarterly earnings revisions page paints a mixed picture. Analyst updates show a split community, with revisions leaning slightly negative on the critical top line despite the stock's surge, as bulls and bears offset each other without clear momentum toward upgrades. 

Wall Street expects the chipmaker to post EPS of $0.08 on revenue of $13.39 billion, implying a fall of 6% during the quarter.

Further down, Wall Street consensus implies just 7% year‑ahead revenue growth, modest at best given the AI hype around Intel's accelerators and foundry buildout, suggesting much of the narrative may already be in the price.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings recommends Intel as a Hold with a score of 3.49 out of 5.

Heading into earnings season, J.P. Morgan said many semiconductor firms appear to have "considerable upside" to the AI accelerator total addressable market.

Momentum metrics on Seeking Alpha support the short‑term strength but lack conviction on sustainability, setting up Intel for a high‑stakes test where a beat could validate the rally and force estimate chasing, while any revenue shortfall or cautious guidance risks a swift unwind.

data.title: BNP Paribas eyes stronger demand for analog; upgrades Texas Instruments, Onsemi, Microchip
data.description:
BNP Paribas has identified a broad-based restocking cycle for semiconductors across industrial end markets, which it believes should provide upside for analog products.

This should drive above-average seasonal growth for the next few quarters, prompting the global financial firm to upgrade several companies, including Texas Instruments (TXN), Onsemi (ON) and Microchip Technology (MCHP). 

Texas Instruments and Onsemi were both upgraded to Neutral from Underperform. BNP also assigned them new price targets of $190 and $62, respectively.

Microchip was upgraded to Outperform from Neutral and assigned a $90 price target.

"Our feedback from CES meetings and recent industry conversations is that a restocking cycle is underway in Industrial semis," said BNP Paribas analyst David O'Connor. "Nine quarters post the Q3'23 analog peak and painful inventory correction, inventories are too low and need to be restocked. A restocking cycle typically takes a few quarters to complete and is likely to drive above seasonal growth."

"This is also a leading indicator of industrial customers feeling better about H2 demand, which bodes well for the broader industrial space," he added. "We believe this restocking is broad-based across technologies and geographies."

About 50% of Microchip's sales stem from the industrial sector, while industrial accounts for 30% of Onsemi's revenue and 34% for Texas Instruments.  

However, O'Connor pointed out that auto semi demand remains low but could see a recovery in 2027. The auto end market represents nearly half of Onsemi's revenue.

"We expect the semi market to grow 18% y/y in 2026, (9 pts from memory), with analog semis growing 10% y/y driven by Industrial restocking," O'Connor noted. "Analog Revenues/EPS remain 25-56% below previous peaks on average, and investors are still underweight. With the next stage of the cyclical recovery now underway, we like the setup and are turning more positive on Analog."

data.title: JBS expands capacity in Saudi Arabia
data.description:
JBS N.V. (JBS) is accelerating its bet on Saudi Arabia with a fresh expansion of its poultry processing operations in Jeddah that will double output from its newest plant by the end of 2026. The development is a positive indication of the scale of local demand and Riyadh's push for food-security-linked industrial investment.

The world’s largest meatpacker has already used the greenfield Jeddah facility, opened last year under its Seara brand, to quadruple its total production capacity in Saudi Arabia compared with its original industrial footprint in Dammam. That has turned Saudi Arabia into a regional hub for JBS (JBS) that now supplies processed chicken products such as nuggets, fingers, and other breaded items to neighboring Gulf markets. 

The production expansion is backed by roughly $50M of capital for the Jeddah plant as part of an $85M nationwide investment program since 2021. The expansion plan is expected to lift JBS' (JBS) processed chicken capacity in Saudi Arabia close to 40,000 tons annually, as well as add roughly 500 direct jobs and take its local headcount close to 1,000. The initiative is also seen as complementing an existing Dammam operation that produces beef burgers, sausages, and other items and a network of eight distribution centers. 

Strategically, the commitment by JBS (JBS) boosts its three-decade commercial relationship with the Saudi market and aligns the Brazilian food giant with the Kingdom’s Vision 2030 agenda.

Shares of JBS (JBS) rose 1.4% in Thursday morning trading. The stock is up more than 7% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: PCE inflation, personal spending growth matches consensus in November
data.description:
November Personal Consumption Expenditure Price Index: +0.2% vs. +0.2% consensus and +0.3% prior, according to data released by the Bureau of Economic Analysis on Thursday. +2.8% Y/Y vs. +2.8% consensus and +2.8% prior.

Core PCE price index, excluding food and energy: +0.2% vs. +0.2% consensus and +0.2% prior. +2.8% Y/Y vs. +2.8% consensus and +2.8% prior.

Personal outlays: +0.5% M/M vs. +0.5% consensus and +0.4% prior.

Personal income: +0.3% vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.1% prior.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Wall Street climbs higher as Greenland fears ease
data.description:
Wall Street shifted higher on Thursday, adding to gains sparked by President Trump reversing course on new tariffs on European countries.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last higher by 0.5%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) gained 0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) had climbed by 0.4%. 

From a sector standpoint, six of the S&P 500’s 11 sectors were in positive trading territory with communication services leading the way. In reverse, utilities have been the weakest performer.

Treasury yields inched up, with the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) up 2 basis points to 3.61%, and the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) up 2 basis points to 4.27%. 

On the economic front, the U.S.'s Q3 GDP was revised to +4.4% from its initial estimate of +4.3%. Furthermore, initial jobless claims for the week ended Jan. 17 edged up by 1K to 200K, trailing the 209K consensus level. 

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Moderna (MRNA) are +6.5%, while shares of Abott Laboratories (ABT) were down 7.2%.

data.title: Rigetti rises after upgrade at B. Riley
data.description:
Shares of Rigetti Computing (RGTI) rose about 4% on Thursday after B. Riley upgraded the shares to Buy from Neutral while keeping the $35 price target on the stock.

Analysts led by Craig Ellis said they are incrementally more encouraged with the near-term roadmap’s commercial trajectory after Rigetti received an $8.4M purchase order to deliver a 108-qubit quantum computer to India's Centre for Development of Advanced Computing, which is 48% and 36% of the consensus 2025 low and high end consensus range, respectively.

"So, with enhanced full-year estimate attainment visibility and 46% PT upside, we upgrade shares from Neutral back to Buy," said Ellis and his team.

The analysts reiterated their Buy ratings on IonQ (IONQ) with a $100 price target and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) with a $33 price target.

data.title: Wells Fargo CEO: ‘Growth to be stronger than people expect’
data.description:
Wells Fargo CEO Charlie Scharf believes U.S. economic growth is poised to exceed market expectations, expressing strong optimism about the country’s financial trajectory.

“It’s more likely than not that growth will be stronger than people expect,” Scharf said during an interview with CNBC in Davos for the World Economic Forum.

The CEO expressed confidence in the U.S. market, noting that the growth trajectory is “really, really good” for the largely domestic-focused company (WFC).

On interest rates, Scharf predicted they “likely will go lower,” which he views as positive for the economy.

Scharf also addressed the Trump administration’s proposal to potentially cap credit card interest rates at 10%. While acknowledging that the president “has not suggested that rates get capped forever across the board,” he emphasized the need for caution when implementing such policies in the credit market.

The Wells Fargo CEO warned against sweeping price controls that could harm consumers who rely on credit access. “10% for everyone overall, I think, would be a real problem,” Scharf said, adding that policymakers should avoid “artificial price controls that could be harmful at a period of time when it matters most to Americans.”

Scharf also weighed in on housing policy, arguing that policies should not make it more advantageous for institutions to purchase homes compared to individual buyers.

“We shouldn’t have a policy that where it is more affordable for an institution to buy something than an individual,” he said.

Lastly, regarding the idea of allowing Americans to use 401(k) funds for home down payments, Scharf called the concept “super interesting” but cautioned that “the devil’s in the details” when it comes to preserving long-term investment integrity.

Bank ETFs: (KBWB), (KBE), and (FTXO).

Regional bank ETFs: (KRE), (KBWR), (IAT), and (DPST).

data.title: BofA's Brian Moynihan echoes concerns over Trump card-cap proposal
data.description:
Bank of America (BAC) CEO Brian Moynihan said Thursday that U.S. President Donald Trump's proposal to cap credit card interest rates would slow consumer spending and restrict access to credit.

“If you actually make this a policy, it can re-allocate credit,” Moynihan said in an interview with Bloomberg Television at the World Economic Forum. “That will slow down spending, it will slow down credit availability, and that might not be what you’re trying to achieve.”

Even so, the CEO acknowledged affordability challenges facing consumers, noting his bank has been discussing the topic with the Trump administration.

Earlier in the month, U.S. President Donald Trump called for a one-year cap on credit card rates at 10%, effective Jan. 20, noting that he will not allow Americans to be “ripped off” by credit card firms whose credit card interest rates have reached 20%–30% or more.

Agreeing with Moyniahn's assessment, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon said a rate cap could choke off credit access for most Americans. Wall Street is widely skeptical that such a move will succeed, if history is any guide.
